items.q: Is an SSD a type of RAM?
items.correct: No - an SSD is non-volatile secondary storage that keeps files when the power is off; RAM is volatile main memory used while programs run.
items.misconception: Yes - SSDs are just large RAM chips, so they do the same job.
items.random_wrong: Yes - installing an SSD increases the computer's RAM.
items.why: Both use chips with no moving parts, so they merge. The dividing line is volatility and role: working memory (RAM) versus permanent storage (SSD).

items.q: Does a physically bigger device always have more storage capacity?
items.correct: No - capacity depends on the storage technology and density, not physical size; a tiny microSD card can hold more than a large old hard drive.
items.misconception: Yes - larger boxes always contain more gigabytes.
items.random_wrong: Yes - storage is measured by weight.
items.why: Physical intuition maps size onto capacity. Flash density has decoupled the two completely - a useful reminder that specifications, not appearances, matter.

items.q: Can you install your own choice of applications on an embedded system?
items.correct: No - embedded systems run fixed, dedicated software for one function; unlike a general-purpose PC, they are not designed for user-installed programs.
items.misconception: Yes - every device with a chip can run any app.
items.random_wrong: Yes - embedded systems include a full app store.
items.why: Smartphones blur the boundary, but the exam definition is dedicated function with fixed firmware - the washing machine cannot become a games console.

items.q: Which storage technology has no moving parts?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Solid-state drive (SSD). “Magnetic hard disk drive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is an example of an embedded system?
items.correct: The microprocessor controlling a washing machine
items.why: It's The microprocessor controlling a washing machine. “A gaming desktop PC” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The data bus is used to:
items.correct: Carry data between the processor, memory and other components
items.misconception: Hold the address of the next instruction
items.why: Correct answer: Carry data between the processor, memory and other components. “Hold the address of the next instruction” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is optical storage?
items.why: It's A Blu-ray disc. “An SSD” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many bits are there in 2 bytes?
items.why: Exact answer: 16 bits. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
